docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/artifacts/story-01/006-verifier-codex53-high.jsonl:7:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_2","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba /Users/leemoore/code/liminal-spec/docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/tech-design.md | sed -n '1,260p'"","aggregated_output":"     1	# Technical Design: ls-claude-impl
     2	
     3	## Purpose
     4	
     5	This design translates the `ls-claude-impl` epic into an implementable architecture for a new Claude Code orchestration skill plus a Node-compatible implementation CLI. The feature has two dense domains that need different levels of treatment: the orchestrator-facing skill surface and the CLI/runtime surface that executes bounded operations for multiple secondary harnesses. For that reason this design uses **Config B**:
     6	
     7	| Document | Purpose |
     8	|---|---|
     9	| `tech-design.md` | Decision record, context, system view, top-tier surfaces, module architecture overview, dependency and packaging decisions, work breakdown |
    10	| `tech-design-skill-process.md` | The orchestrator-facing skill surface: progressive disclosure docs, reading journey, state ownership, process playbook, stage transitions |
    11	| `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` | The CLI/runtime surface: command model, config schemas, prompt asset system, provider adapters, operation contracts, packaging |
    12	| `test-plan.md` | TC-to-test mapping, mock strategy, fixture strategy, test counts, chunk-level TDD plan |
    13	
    14	The index stays small enough to be navigable while still recording the cross-cutting decisions that both companion docs inherit. The companion split follows the real boundary in this feature: one side teaches the orchestrator how the process works, the other side executes the process through a clean agent-first CLI.
    15	
    16	## Spec Validation
    17	
    18	The epic is designable. Its major flows, ACs, and TCs describe a coherent system with clear public seams: spec-pack discovery, run configuration, prompt assembly, retained implementation sessions, fresh verification, cleanup, and epic closeout. The epic is unusually detailed for a skill feature, but the added specificity is appropriate because the feature is not just a prose skill; it is a skill plus a runtime helper plus a prompt system plus a result-contract surface.
    19	
    20	The design does make three clarifications that should be recorded explicitly before implementation begins. None of them block design, but each one turns an implied behavior into a concrete contract the implementation can build around.
    21	
    22	| Issue | Spec Location | Resolution | Status |
    23	|---|---|---|---|
    24	| The epic requires the orchestrator to record role defaults and run choices, but it does not name a dedicated machine-readable run config file. | AC-2.4, Data Contracts, `team-impl-log.md` requirements | Introduce `impl-run.config.json` at the spec-pack root as the orchestrator-owned run configuration. `team-impl-log.md` remains the durable narrative/state record. | Resolved — clarified |
    25	| The epic distinguishes Codex/Copilot-backed GPT lanes from Claude-only fallback, but the harness terminology needs to preserve Claude Code as the always-present primary harness. | AC-2.2, AC-2.3, Role Model Matrix | Use `primary_harness: "claude-code"` plus per-role `secondary_harness: "codex" | "copilot" | "none"`. `none` means the role uses the primary harness only. | Resolved — clarified |
    26	| The epic says the CLI should be stateless across calls, but story implementation also requires retained implementor continuity. | Constraint C3, AC-4.2, AC-6.3 | Keep orchestration state outside the CLI. The CLI may emit continuation/session handles for provider sessions, but the orchestrator owns persistence, routing, and recovery through `team-impl-log.md`, `impl-run.config.json`, and any future orchestrator-managed support files. | Resolved — clarified |
    27	| The epic allows Copilot as the generic GPT fallback, but v1 Copilot support is limited to fresh-session roles while story implementation requires retained continuation. | AC-2.2, AC-4.2, Role Model Matrix | In v1, disallow Copilot for `story_implementor`. Retained implementor fallback order is Codex -> Claude Code primary harness. Copilot remains valid for fresh verifier, quick-fix, and synthesis roles. | Resolved — deviated |
    28	| The epic requires the packaged skill to include the CLI, but the current build surface only composes markdown skill outputs and bundled references. | Feature overview, CLI responsibilities implied by scope | Add a build step that bundles the modular CLI source into a single Node-compatible JS file and copies that file into the packaged skill output. | Resolved — deviated |
    29	
    30	### Tech Design Question Coverage
    31	
    32	The epic’s Tech Design Questions are answered by this pack as follows:
    33	
    34	| Question | Primary Answer Location |
    35	|---|---|
    36	| Public CLI commands, arguments, exit codes, result formats | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Command Surface, Result Contracts |
    37	| Prompt assets storage and assembly | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Prompt Asset System |
    38	| Three self-review passes | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Story Implementation Flow |
    39	| Model matrix and harness fallback | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Run Config + Provider Adapters |
    40	| Continuation identifiers | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Session Continuation Contracts |
    41	| Exact result fields and serialization | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Result Contracts |
    42	| `team-impl-log.md` state model | `tech-design-skill-process.md` — Orchestrator State and Durable Artifacts |
    43	| How orchestrator consumes CLI results | `tech-design-skill-process.md` — Process Playbook and Stage Transitions |
    44	| Verification-gate hardening | `tech-design.md` — Verification Scripts; `tech-design-skill-process.md` — Initialization Flow |
    45	| Companion tech-design naming convention | This index — document structure and companion naming |
    46	| Implementor replacement vs continuation | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Story Continue Rules |
    47	| Quick-fix narrow contract | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Quick Fix Workflow |
    48	| Pre-epic cleanup artifacts | `tech-design-skill-process.md` and `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Epic Cleanup |
    49	| Self-review prompt evolution and stop rules | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Story Implementation Flow |
    50	
    51	## Context
    52	
    53	`liminal-spec` is primarily a content-and-build repository, not a general application runtime. Most skills in the repo are markdown-first artifacts composed from `src/` and packaged into installable skill folders or standalone markdown files. That matters because `ls-claude-impl` is not a normal addition to the pack. It introduces a second kind of deliverable inside the same repo: a bundled runtime helper that must ship inside the packaged skill while still fitting the repository’s content-composition workflow.
    54	
    55	The problem being solved is reliability of implementation orchestration, not lack of methodology. Existing implementation skill surfaces carry useful process ideas, but they rely on too much live runtime improvisation and too many layers between the orchestrator and the actual work. The new design narrows the system to three stable public surfaces. First, the skill teaches the orchestrator the process and the reading journey. Second, the orchestrator owns state and decision-making. Third, the CLI executes bounded operations and returns disciplined machine-readable results. The center of gravity shifts away from “agent teams all the way down” and toward “one orchestrator with explicit process docs and a legible execution surface.”
    56	
    57	The future direction also matters now. The CLI is not just a local helper for this skill. It is an early public execution surface that is expected to map cleanly into a later SDK and then into web-app harness tools. That creates strong pressure toward explicit inputs, outputs, and outcome states. Hidden orchestration state inside the CLI would make later extraction harder. For the same reason, the skill should own the explanation of the process while the CLI owns execution. The orchestrator needs to understand what is happening at the process level without carrying implementation detail that belongs inside the runtime.
    58	
    59	A second pressure comes from prompt architecture. This feature does not only need CLI commands; it needs stable role prompts, reusable snippets, public insert hooks, and role-fit reading journeys. Those prompt assets are implementation-critical. They are not just docs, and they are not just helper strings. They are part of the runtime contract. The design therefore treats the prompt system as a first-class architectural surface rather than as glue hidden inside an implementation file.
    60	
    61	Finally, the repo’s existing build pipeline constrains how the feature lands. The current build emits markdown skill directories plus standalone markdown packs. It does not yet package executable runtime files into skill outputs. The design therefore includes build-system changes as part of the feature instead of leaving them to Story 0 discovery. The packaged skill must contain everything the orchestrator needs at runtime: markdown guidance plus a single bundled Node-compatible CLI file.
    62

   105	### External Contracts
   106	
   107	The external contracts are narrower than the whole feature. They are the fixed points the orchestrator and CLI must agree on before implementation begins.
   108	
   109	#### Incoming Contracts
   110	
   111	| Input | Required | Source | Purpose |
   112	|---|---|---|---|
   113	| `epic.md` | yes | spec-pack root | Functional source of truth |
   114	| `tech-design.md` | yes | spec-pack root | Design index consumed by the orchestrator and some CLI operations |
   115	| `test-plan.md` | yes | spec-pack root | TC→test mapping and verification strategy |
   116	| `tech-design-*.md` companions | conditional | spec-pack root | Required only when the tech-design config is 4-doc |
   117	| `stories/` | yes | spec-pack root | Ordered story slices for implementation |
   118	| `impl-run.config.json` | yes | orchestrator-authored | Role/harness/model configuration for runtime operations |
   119	| `custom-story-impl-prompt-insert.md` | no | spec-pack root | Optional public insert for implementor prompt assembly |
   120	| `custom-story-verifier-prompt-insert.md` | no | spec-pack root | Optional public insert for verifier prompt assembly |
   121	
   122	#### Outgoing Contracts
   123	
   124	| Output | Producer | Destination | Purpose |
   125	|---|---|---|---|
   126	| `team-impl-log.md` updates | orchestrator | spec-pack root | Durable orchestration record and recovery state |
   127	| Structured command result JSON | CLI | stdout / orchestrator ingest | Machine-readable completion surface for each operation |
   128	| Prompt-ready execution payloads | CLI | secondary harnesses | Stable provider-facing prompt contract |
   129	| Bundled CLI artifact | build surface | packaged skill output | Runtime executable shipped with the skill |
   130	
   131	#### Outcome States
   132	
   133	The CLI should use explicit outcome states instead of ambiguous free-text status messages.
   134	
   135	| Operation Class | Allowed Outcome States |
   136	|---|---|
   137	| `inspect`, `preflight` | `ready`, `needs-user-decision`, `blocked` |
   138	| implementation / quick-fix operations | `ready-for-verification`, `needs-followup-fix`, `needs-human-ruling`, `blocked` |
   139	| verifier batches | `pass`, `revise`, `block` |
   140	| cleanup operations | `cleaned`, `needs-more-cleanup`, `blocked` |
   141	| synthesis operations | `ready-for-closeout`, `needs-fixes`, `needs-more-verification`, `blocked` |
   142	
   143	#### Runtime Prerequisites
   144	
   145	| Prerequisite | Where Needed | How to Verify |
   146	|---|---|---|
   147	| Bun 1.3.x+ | local development, build, tests | `bun --version` |
   148	| Node-compatible runtime | packaged CLI execution | `node -v` |
   149	| Git repository root | CLI commands that inspect repo/worktree context | `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` |
   150	| `codex` CLI | only when a role uses `secondary_harness: "codex"` | `codex --version` plus auth/smoke command |
   151	| `copilot` CLI | only when a role uses `secondary_harness: "copilot"` | `copilot --version` plus auth/smoke command |
   152	| `claude` CLI | always, because primary harness is Claude Code | `claude --version` plus auth/smoke command |

   252	The key boundary is that the orchestrator uses the skill to understand what should happen, then uses the CLI to make one bounded thing happen, then writes the result into its own durable state. The CLI does not own orchestration lifecycle between calls.

   254	### Manifest and Build Metadata
   255	
   256	The build surface needs one new manifest-level concept: a bundled runtime artifact that belongs inside the packaged skill even though it does not belong inside the composed markdown body.
   257	
   258	Proposed shape:
   259	
   260	```json
","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/artifacts/story-01/006-verifier-codex53-high.jsonl:11:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_4","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba /Users/leemoore/code/liminal-spec/docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/tech-design-cli-runtime.md | sed -n '1,320p'"","aggregated_output":"     1	# Technical Design Companion: CLI Runtime Surface
     2	
     3	## Purpose
     4	
     5	This companion covers the `ls-impl-cli` runtime that executes bounded operations on behalf of the `ls-claude-impl` orchestrator. The CLI is not the methodology. It is the execution surface that makes the methodology practical. It validates readiness, assembles provider-facing prompts, calls secondary harnesses when configured, captures structured outputs, and returns disciplined result contracts to the orchestrator.
     6	
     7	The design goal is an agent-first CLI. Every command should correspond to a clear process action that the orchestrator already understands from the skill docs. The CLI should hide internal complexity but not produce mystery. It should accept explicit inputs, return explicit results, and avoid holding orchestration lifecycle in hidden runtime state between calls.
     8	
     9	## Runtime Surface Overview
    10	
    11	The runtime has five responsibilities:
    12	
    13	1. Resolve and validate the spec-pack surface.
    14	2. Load and validate the orchestrator-owned run config.
    15	3. Assemble prompts from embedded base prompts, snippets, runtime values, and public inserts.
    16	4. Execute one bounded operation through the appropriate primary/secondary harness combination.
    17	5. Return structured results that the orchestrator can persist and act on.
    18	
    19	The runtime does **not** own:
    20	
    21	- story progression
    22	- acceptance decisions
    23	- cumulative baselines across the whole run
    24	- cleanup review decisions
    25	- epic closeout decisions
    26	
    27	Those remain with the orchestrator.
    28	
    29	## Shared Result Envelope
    30	
    31	Every command should return the same outer JSON envelope so the orchestrator can ingest results without command-specific parsing rules.
    32	
    33	```typescript
    34	export interface CliError {
    35	  code: string;
    36	  message: string;
    37	  detail?: string;
    38	}
    39	
    40	export interface CliArtifactRef {
    41	  kind: string;
    42	  path: string;
    43	}
    44	
    45	export interface CliResultEnvelope<T> {
    46	  command: string;
    47	  version: 1;
    48	  status: "ok" | "needs-user-decision" | "blocked" | "error";
    49	  outcome: string;
    50	  result?: T;
    51	  errors: CliError[];
    52	  warnings: string[];
    53	  artifacts: CliArtifactRef[];
    54	  startedAt: string;
    55	  finishedAt: string;
    56	}
    57	```
    58	
    59	The JSON envelope is the source of truth. Human-readable `stderr` is allowed for progress or debugging, but the orchestrator should route decisions from the envelope rather than from ad hoc terminal text.
    60	
    61	Suggested stable error codes:
    62	
    63	- `INVALID_SPEC_PACK`
    64	- `INVALID_RUN_CONFIG`
    65	- `VERIFICATION_GATE_UNRESOLVED`
    66	- `PROVIDER_UNAVAILABLE`
    67	- `PROVIDER_OUTPUT_INVALID`
    68	- `CONTINUATION_HANDLE_INVALID`
    69	- `PROMPT_ASSET_MISSING`
    70	- `PROMPT_INSERT_INVALID`
    71	
    72	## Public CLI Contract
    73	
    74	The public CLI contract consists of:
    75	
    76	1. command signatures in `Command Signatures`
    77	2. shared JSON envelope in `Shared Result Envelope`
    78	3. stdout/stderr/artifact behavior in `Public CLI IO Contract`
    79	4. exit-code and routing behavior in `Exit Codes` and `Result Routing Matrix`
    80	5. provider execution assumptions in `Provider Invocation Contracts`
    81	
    82	An implementation that matches those five surfaces should be interchangeable at the orchestrator boundary even if internal module structure changes later.
    83	
    84	## Command Surface
    85	
    86	The public CLI surface should be small and stable.
    87	
    88	| Command | Purpose | Required Inputs | Primary Outputs | Outcome States |
    89	|---|---|---|---|---|
    90	| `inspect` | Resolve spec-pack layout and story inventory | spec-pack root | spec-pack summary, story order, insert presence | `ready`, `needs-user-decision`, `blocked` |
    91	| `preflight` | Validate run config, provider availability, prompt assets, and run readiness | spec-pack root, `impl-run.config.json` | readiness report, validated config, provider matrix | `ready`, `needs-user-decision`, `blocked` |
    92	| `story-implement` | Start implementation for one story | story id, spec-pack root, run config | implementor result, continuation handle | `ready-for-verification`, `needs-followup-fix`, `needs-human-ruling`, `blocked` |
    93	| `story-continue` | Continue a retained implementor session for the current story | continuation handle, follow-up request | continued implementor result | `ready-for-verification`, `needs-followup-fix`, `needs-human-ruling`, `blocked` |
    94	| `story-verify` | Run a fresh verifier batch for one story | story id, run config, verification context | verifier batch result array | `pass`, `revise`, `block` |
    95	| `quick-fix` | Apply a narrow, bounded fix request | fix request, affected scope, role config | quick-fix result | `ready-for-verification`, `needs-more-routing`, `blocked` |
    96	| `epic-cleanup` | Apply cleanup-only corrections before epic verification | cleanup batch, selected fix workflow | cleanup result | `cleaned`, `needs-more-cleanup`, `blocked` |
    97	| `epic-verify` | Run fresh epic-level verification | spec-pack root, run config | epic verifier result array | `pass`, `revise`, `block` |
    98	| `epic-synthesize` | Verify and consolidate epic-level findings | epic verifier results, run config | synthesis result | `ready-for-closeout`, `needs-fixes`, `needs-more-verification`, `blocked` |
    99	
   100	The CLI with no arguments should print quick-start help only. The full process explanation stays in the skill docs.
   101	
   102	### Command Signatures
   103	
   104	The public signatures should stay stable even if internal implementation changes.
   105	
   106	| Command | Signature |
   107	|---|---|
   108	| `inspect` | `ls-impl-cli inspect --spec-pack-root <path> [--json]` |
   109	| `preflight` | `ls-impl-cli preflight --spec-pack-root <path> [--config <path>] [--story-gate <cmd>] [--epic-gate <cmd>] [--json]` |
   110	| `story-implement` | `ls-impl-cli story-implement --spec-pack-root <path> --story-id <id> [--config <path>] [--json]` |
   111	| `story-continue` | `ls-impl-cli story-continue --spec-pack-root <path> --story-id <id> --provider <provider> --session-id <id> [--config <path>] [--followup-file <path> | --followup-text <text>] [--json]` |
   112	| `story-verify` | `ls-impl-cli story-verify --spec-pack-root <path> --story-id <id> [--config <path>] [--json]` |
   113	| `quick-fix` | `ls-impl-cli quick-fix --spec-pack-root <path> --story-id <id> --request-file <path> [--scope-file <path>] [--config <path>] [--json]` |
   114	| `epic-cleanup` | `ls-impl-cli epic-cleanup --spec-pack-root <path> --cleanup-batch <path> [--config <path>] [--json]` |
   115	| `epic-verify` | `ls-impl-cli epic-verify --spec-pack-root <path> [--config <path>] [--json]` |
   116	| `epic-synthesize` | `ls-impl-cli epic-synthesize --spec-pack-root <path> --verifier-report <path> --verifier-report <path> [--config <path>] [--json]` |
   117	
   118	All commands should support `--json` for machine-readable output. Human-readable summaries may exist for manual use, but the orchestrator contract should be the JSON payload.
   119	
   120	### Public CLI IO Contract
   121	
   122	The CLI should behave consistently across commands.
   123	
   124	| Surface | Rule |
   125	|---|---|
   126	| stdout with `--json` | emit exactly one `CliResultEnvelope<T>` JSON object |
   127	| stdout without `--json` | emit a concise human-readable summary only |
   128	| stderr | reserved for progress/debugging; never required for orchestration routing |
   129	| persisted artifacts | write the same JSON envelope that was emitted on stdout |
   130	| default config path | `<spec-pack-root>/impl-run.config.json` unless `--config` overrides it |
   131	| default artifact root | `<spec-pack-root>/artifacts/` |
   132	
   133	### Command Examples
   134	
   135	```bash
   136	node bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs inspect --spec-pack-root /abs/spec-pack --json
   137	node bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs preflight --spec-pack-root /abs/spec-pack --json
   138	node bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs story-implement --spec-pack-root /abs/spec-pack --story-id story-03 --json
   139	node bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs story-verify --spec-pack-root /abs/spec-pack --story-id story-03 --json
   140	node bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs epic-cleanup --spec-pack-root /abs/spec-pack --cleanup-batch /abs/spec-pack/artifacts/cleanup/cleanup-batch.md --json
   141	```
   142	
   143	For commands whose inner payload also contains an `outcome`, the envelope `outcome` is the routing source of truth and must match the inner payload exactly. Schema validation should reject mismatches.
   144	
   145	### Exit Codes
   146	
   147	Exit codes are a coarse routing signal. The JSON payload remains the source of truth.
   148	
   149	| Exit Code | Meaning |
   150	|---|---|
   151	| `0` | Command completed and produced a non-blocking outcome (`ready`, `ready-for-verification`, `pass`, `cleaned`, `ready-for-closeout`) |
   152	| `2` | Command completed but requires follow-up (`needs-user-decision`, `needs-followup-fix`, `needs-human-ruling`, `revise`, `needs-more-routing`, `needs-more-cleanup`, `needs-fixes`, `needs-more-verification`) |
   153	| `3` | Command is blocked by environment, spec-pack, config, or provider readiness issue |
   154	| `1` | Invalid invocation, schema parse failure, or uncaught runtime error |
   155	
   156	### Result Routing Matrix
   157	
   158	This matrix is authoritative for handler implementation and tests.
   159	
   160	| Command Outcome | Envelope Status | Exit Code | Orchestrator Action |
   161	|---|---|---:|---|
   162	| `ready` | `ok` | 0 | proceed to next setup step |
   163	| `ready-for-verification` | `ok` | 0 | run verification or next bounded step |
   164	| `pass` | `ok` | 0 | evaluate for acceptance or next stage |
   165	| `cleaned` | `ok` | 0 | move into epic verification |
   166	| `ready-for-closeout` | `ok` | 0 | run final orchestrator-owned gate |
   167	| `needs-user-decision` | `needs-user-decision` | 2 | pause and request human clarification |
   168	| `needs-followup-fix` | `ok` | 2 | route same-session follow-up or fix path |
   169	| `needs-human-ruling` | `needs-user-decision` | 2 | pause for orchestrator/human ruling |
   170	| `revise` | `ok` | 2 | route fix and rerun verification |
   171	| `needs-more-routing` | `ok` | 2 | select another bounded correction path |
   172	| `needs-more-cleanup` | `ok` | 2 | continue cleanup cycle |
   173	| `needs-fixes` | `ok` | 2 | route fixes before closeout |
   174	| `needs-more-verification` | `ok` | 2 | launch additional verification/synthesis |
   175	| `blocked` | `blocked` | 3 | stop and inspect blocking error details |
   176	| parse/schema/runtime failure | `error` | 1 | treat as CLI failure rather than feature-state outcome |

   207	│   └── embedded-assets.generated.ts
   208	└── prompts/
   209	    ├── base/
   210	    └── snippets/
   211	```
   212	
   213	The command modules stay thin. They parse command-specific flags with `citty`, call into core services, and print structured JSON. All real behavior lives in `core/`.
   214	
   215	### Core Service Interfaces
   216	
   217	The command handlers should depend on a small set of explicit core interfaces.
   218	
   219	```typescript
   220	export interface SpecPackResolver {
   221	  resolve(specPackRoot: string): Promise<InspectResult>;
   222	}
   223	
   224	export interface VerificationGateResolver {
   225	  resolve(input: {
   226	    specPackRoot: string;
   227	    explicitStoryGate?: string;
   228	    explicitEpicGate?: string;
   229	  }): Promise<VerificationGateConfig | CliError>;
   230	}
   231	
   232	export interface ArtifactWriter {
   233	  write<T>(params: {
   234	    specPackRoot: string;
   235	    command: string;
   236	    storyId?: string;
   237	    payload: CliResultEnvelope<T>;
   238	  }): Promise<CliArtifactRef>;
   239	}
   240	
   241	export interface ProviderResultParser<T> {
   242	  parse(raw: ProviderExecutionResult): Promise<T | CliError>;
   243	}
   244	```
   245	
   246	These interfaces are deliberately smaller than the whole runtime. They pin the boundaries that are most likely to drift during implementation.
   247	
   248	## Run Configuration Contract
   249	
   250	The CLI consumes an explicit orchestrator-owned JSON file. The contract is intentionally lean.
   251	
   252	```typescript
   253	export type PrimaryHarness = "claude-code";
   254	export type SecondaryHarness = "codex" | "copilot" | "none";
   255	export type ReasoningEffort = "low" | "medium" | "high" | "xhigh";
   256	
   257	export interface RoleAssignment {
   258	  secondary_harness: SecondaryHarness;
   259	  model: string;
   260	  reasoning_effort: ReasoningEffort;
   261	}
   262	
   263	export interface EpicVerifierAssignment extends RoleAssignment {
   264	  label: string;
   265	}
   266	
   267	export interface ImplRunConfig {
   268	  version: 1;
   269	  primary_harness: PrimaryHarness;
   270	  story_implementor: RoleAssignment;
   271	  quick_fixer: RoleAssignment;
   272	  story_verifier_1: RoleAssignment;
   273	  story_verifier_2: RoleAssignment;
   274	  self_review: {
   275	    passes: number;
   276	  };
   277	  epic_verifiers: EpicVerifierAssignment[];
   278	  epic_synthesizer: RoleAssignment;
   279	}
   280	```
   281	
   282	Canonical display labels:
   283	
   284	| Stored Value | Display Label |
   285	|---|---|
   286	| `low` | low |
   287	| `medium` | medium |
   288	| `high` | high |
   289	| `xhigh` | extra high |
   290	
   291	`c12` should be used in explicit-file mode for this feature. The CLI should load either the path passed via `--config` or the default `impl-run.config.json` in the spec-pack root. It should not enable multi-source discovery from home config, `package.json`, or environment overlays in v1. The orchestrator benefits from one obvious config file and one obvious precedence model.
   292	
   293	### Validation Rules
   294	
   295	| Rule | Reason |
   296	|---|---|
   297	| `primary_harness` must be `claude-code` in v1 | v1 always runs inside Claude Code |
   298	| `secondary_harness` must be `codex`, `copilot`, or `none` | keep the contract explicit and narrow |
   299	| `none` means “use primary harness” | avoids extra indirection or derived config fields |
   300	| `story_implementor.secondary_harness !== "copilot"` in v1 | Copilot continuation is not supported for the retained implementor role |
   301	| `self_review.passes` must be `>= 1` and `<= 5` | prevents nonsensical or runaway values |
   302	| `epic_verifiers.length >= 1` | epic verification is mandatory |
   303	| duplicate epic verifier labels are invalid | makes downstream reporting legible |
   304	
   305	### Provider Availability Matrix
   306	
   307	The CLI must validate not just schema shape, but whether the local machine can honor the requested harness assignments.
   308	
   309	```typescript
   310	export interface HarnessAvailability {
   311	  harness: SecondaryHarness | PrimaryHarness;
   312	  available: boolean;
   313	  version?: string;
   314	  authStatus?: "authenticated" | "unknown" | "missing";
   315	  notes: string[];
   316	}
   317	
   318	export interface ProviderMatrix {
   319	  primary: HarnessAvailability;
   320	  secondary: HarnessAvailability[];

     3	## Purpose
     4	
     5	This plan maps every TC from the `ls-claude-impl` epic to a concrete test surface. The feature spans both content and runtime, so the test plan does too. Some requirements are verified through packaged skill-content tests because the skill is the source of process truth. Others are verified through CLI command-entrypoint tests because the CLI is the execution surface. Together they cover the full chain:
     6	
     7	```text
     8	Epic AC -> TC -> skill/runtime test -> implementation
     9	```
    10	
    11	The main testing principle is the same one used elsewhere in the methodology: test at public seams. For this feature the public seams are:
    12	
    13	- packaged skill files
    14	- CLI command entrypoints
    15	- provider adapter boundaries
    16	- bundled runtime artifact
    17	
    18	Some requirements in this feature are intentionally **process-contract only** rather than runtime-enforced. Those are validated through skill-content and CLI-guide tests because the orchestrator, not the CLI, owns that behavior. Wherever a requirement is covered that way, the coverage note should be read as “documentation/process contract,” not “hard runtime enforcement.”

    20	## Testing Strategy
    21	
    22	### Test Pyramid for This Feature
    23	
    24	```text
    25	         /\
    26	        /  \  Manual verification
    27	       /----\  - load skill, follow setup, run packaged CLI on fixture spec pack
    28	      /      \
    29	     /--------\  Command-entrypoint tests
    30	    /          \  - inspect, preflight, story-implement, story-verify, quick-fix,
    31	   /------------\    epic-cleanup, epic-verify, epic-synthesize
    32	  /              \
    33	 /----------------\  Schema / prompt assembly / provider adapter tests
    34	/                  \  - config parsing, result contracts, asset embedding, adapter rules
    35	```
    36	
    37	### Critical Mocking Rule
    38	
    39	**Mock only external boundaries.**
    40	
    41	For this feature that means:
    42	
    43	| Boundary | Mock? | Why |
    44	|---|---|---|
    45	| provider CLIs (`claude`, `codex`, `copilot`) | Yes | external process boundary |
    46	| auth / environment availability checks | Yes | external machine state |
    47	| filesystem fixture content | No | spec-pack resolution is core behavior and should run on real temp dirs |
    48	| prompt assembly internals | No | that is feature logic, not a boundary |
    49	| command routing modules | No | command entrypoints should exercise internal orchestration for real |
    50	
    51	The command tests should run against real fixture directories on disk and real command handlers. Only process spawning and harness availability should be mocked or faked.
    52

additional verification/synthesis |\\n   175\\t| `blocked` | `blocked` | 3 | stop and inspect blocking error details |\\n   176\\t| parse/schema/runtime failure | `error` | 1 | treat as CLI failure rather than feature-state outcome |\\n   177\\t\\n   178\\t## Runtime Module Layout\\n   179\\t\\n   180\\t```text\\n   181\\tprocesses/impl-cli/\\n   182\\t├── cli.ts\\n   183\\t├── commands/\\n   184\\t│   ├── inspect.ts\\n   185\\t│   ├── preflight.ts\\n   186\\t│   ├── story-implement.ts\\n   187\\t│   ├── story-continue.ts\\n   188\\t│   ├── story-verify.ts\\n   189\\t│   ├── quick-fix.ts\\n   190\\t│   ├── epic-cleanup.ts\\n   191\\t│   ├── epic-verify.ts\\n   192\\t│   └── epic-synthesize.ts\\n   193\\t├── core/\\n   194\\t│   ├── config-schema.ts\\n   195\\t│   ├── spec-pack.ts\\n   196\\t│   ├── story-order.ts\\n   197\\t│   ├── prompt-assets.ts\\n   198\\t│   ├── prompt-assembly.ts\\n   199\\t│   ├── provider-checks.ts\\n   200\\t│   ├── provider-adapters/\\n   201\\t│   │   ├── claude-code.ts\\n   202\\t│   │   ├── codex.ts\\n   203\\t│   │   └── copilot.ts\\n   204\\t│   ├── result-contracts.ts\\n   205\\t│   ├── status-codes.ts\\n   206\\t│   ├── command-context.ts\\n   207\\t│   └── embedded-assets.generated.ts\\n   208\\t└── prompts/\\n   209\\t    ├── base/\\n   210\\t    └── snippets/\\n   211\\t```\\n   212\\t\\n   213\\tThe command modules stay thin. They parse command-specific flags with `citty`, call into core services, and print structured JSON. All real behavior lives in `core/`.\\n   214\\t\\n   215\\t### Core Service Interfaces\\n   216\\t\\n   217\\tThe command handlers should depend on a small set of explicit core interfaces.\\n   218\\t\\n   219\\t```typescript\\n   220\\texport interface SpecPackResolver {\\n   221\\t  resolve(specPackRoot: string): Promise<InspectResult>;\\n   222\\t}\\n   223\\t\\n   224\\texport interface VerificationGateResolver {\\n   225\\t  resolve(input: {\\n   226\\t    specPackRoot: string;\\n   227\\t    explicitStoryGate?: string;\\n   228\\t    explicitEpicGate?: string;\\n   229\\t  }): Promise<VerificationGateConfig | CliError>;\\n   230\\t}\\n   231\\t\\n   232\\texport interface ArtifactWriter {\\n   233\\t  write<T>(params: {\\n   234\\t    specPackRoot: string;\\n   235\\t    command: string;\\n   236\\t    storyId?: string;\\n   237\\t    payload: CliResultEnvelope<T>;\\n   238\\t  }): Promise<CliArtifactRef>;\\n   239\\t}\\n   240\\t\\n   241\\texport interface ProviderResultParser<T> {\\n   242\\t  parse(raw: ProviderExecutionResult): Promise<T | CliError>;\\n   243\\t}\\n   244\\t```\\n   245\\t\\n   246\\tThese interfaces are deliberately smaller than the whole runtime. They pin the boundaries that are most likely to drift during implementation.\\n   247\\t\\n   248\\t## Run Configuration Contract\\n   249\\t\\n   250\\tThe CLI consumes an explicit orchestrator-owned JSON file. The contract is intentionally lean.\\n   251\\t\\n   252\\t```typescript\\n   253\\texport type PrimaryHarness = \\\"claude-code\\\";\\n   254\\texport type SecondaryHarness = \\\"codex\\\" | \\\"copilot\\\" | \\\"none\\\";\\n   255\\texport type ReasoningEffort = \\\"low\\\" | \\\"medium\\\" | \\\"high\\\" | \\\"xhigh\\\";\\n   256\\t\\n   257\\texport interface RoleAssignment {\\n   258\\t  secondary_harness: SecondaryHarness;\\n   259\\t  model: string;\\n   260\\t  reasoning_effort: ReasoningEffort;\\n   261\\t}\\n   262\\t\\n   263\\texport interface EpicVerifierAssignment extends RoleAssignment {\\n   264\\t  label: string;\\n   265\\t}\\n   266\\t\\n   267\\texport interface ImplRunConfig {\\n   268\\t  version: 1;\\n   269\\t  primary_harness: PrimaryHarness;\\n   270\\t  story_implementor: RoleAssignment;\\n   271\\t  quick_fixer: RoleAssignment;\\n   272\\t  story_verifier_1: RoleAssignment;\\n   273\\t  story_verifier_2: RoleAssignment;\\n   274\\t  self_review: {\\n   275\\t    passes: number;\\n   276\\t  };\\n   277\\t  epic_verifiers: EpicVerifierAssignment[];\\n   278\\t  epic_synthesizer: RoleAssignment;\\n   279\\t}\\n   280\\t```\\n   281\\t\\n   282\\tCanonical display labels:\\n   283\\t\\n   284\\t| Stored Value | Display Label |\\n   285\\t|---|---|\\n   286\\t| `low` | low |\\n   287\\t| `medium` | medium |\\n   288\\t| `high` | high |\\n   289\\t| `xhigh` | extra high |\\n   290\\t\\n   291\\t`c12` should be used in explicit-file mode for this feature. The CLI should load either the path passed via `--config` or the default `impl-run.config.json` in the spec-pack root. It should not enable multi-source discovery from home config, `package.json`, or environment overlays in v1. The orchestrator benefits from one obvious config file and one obvious precedence model.\\n   292\\t\\n   293\\t### Validation Rules\\n   294\\t\\n   295\\t| Rule | Reason |\\n   296\\t|---|---|\\n   297\\t| `primary_harness` must be `claude-code` in v1 | v1 always runs inside Claude Code |\\n   298\\t| `secondary_harness` must be `codex`, `copilot`, or `none` | keep the contract explicit and narrow |\\n   299\\t| `none` means “use primary harness” | avoids extra indirection or derived config fields |\\n   300\\t| `story_implementor.secondary_harness !== \\\"copilot\\\"` in v1 | Copilot continuation is not supported for the retained implementor role |\\n   301\\t| `self_review.passes` must be `>= 1` and `<= 5` | prevents nonsensical or runaway values |\\n   302\\t| `epic_verifiers.length >= 1` | epic verification is mandatory |\\n   303\\t| duplicate epic verifier labels are invalid | makes downstream reporting legible |\\n   304\\t\\n   305\\t### Provider Availability Matrix\\n   306\\t\\n   307\\tThe CLI must validate not just schema shape, but whether the local machine can honor the requested harness assignments.\\n   308\\t\\n   309\\t```typescript\\n   310\\texport interface HarnessAvailability {\\n   311\\t  harness: SecondaryHarness | PrimaryHarness;\\n   312\\t  available: boolean;\\n   313\\t  version?: string;\\n   314\\t  authStatus?: \\\"authenticated\\\" | \\\"unknown\\\" | \\\"missing\\\";\\n   315\\t  notes: string[];\\n   316\\t}\\n   317\\t\\n   318\\texport interface ProviderMatrix {\\n   319\\t  primary: HarnessAvailability;\\n   320\\t  secondary: HarnessAvailability[];\\n   321\\t}\\n   322\\t```\\n   323\\t\\n   324\\tValidation behavior:\\n   325\\t\\n   326\\t- Always validate `claude-code` availability because it is the primary harness.\\n   327\\t- Validate `codex` only if at least one role requests `secondary_harness: \\\"codex\\\"`.\\n   328\\t- Validate `copilot` only if at least one role requests `secondary_harness: \\\"copilot\\\"`.\\n   329\\t- Skip provider-CLI checks for roles using `secondary_harness: \\\"none\\\"`.\\n   330\\t\\n   331\\t## Prompt Asset System\\n   332\\t\\n   333\\tPrompt assets are runtime architecture, not incidental text blobs. They should live in source form for maintainability, then be embedded into a generated TS module so the packaged runtime can ship as a single JS file.\\n   334\\t\\n   335\\t### Prompt Asset Layout\\n   336\\t\\n   337\\t```text\\n   338\\tprocesses/impl-cli/prompts/\\n   339\\t├── base/\\n   340\\t│   ├── story-implementor.md\\n   341\\t│   ├── story-verifier.md\\n   342\\t│   ├── quick-fixer.md\\n   343\\t│   ├── epic-verifier.md\\n   344\\t│   └── epic-synthesizer.md\\n   345\\t└── snippets/\\n   346\\t    ├── reading-journey.md\\n   347\\t    ├── report-contract.md\\n   348\\t    ├── gate-instructions.md\\n   349\\t    ├── mock-audit.md\\n   350\\t    ├── self-review-pass-1.md\\n   351\\t    ├── self-review-pass-2.md\\n   352\\t    └── self-review-pass-3.md\\n   353\\t```\\n   354\\t\\n   355\\t### Prompt Asset Content Contracts\\n   356\\t\\n   357\\tEach prompt asset should have a required internal contract, not just a filename.\\n   358\\t\\n   359\\t| Asset | Required Content |\\n   360\\t|---|---|\\n   361\\t| `story-implementor.md` | role stance, artifact reading order, implementation scope, self-review rules, JSON result contract |\\n   362\\t| `story-verifier.md` | evidence rules, severity enum, AC/TC coverage expectations, mock/shim audit rules, routing guidance |\\n   363\\t| `quick-fixer.md` | narrow-scope rule, forbidden full-story reimplementation rule, result contract |\\n   364\\t| `epic-verifier.md` | cross-story checks, architecture consistency, production-path mock/shim audit, verifier result contract |\\n   365\\t| `epic-synthesizer.md` | independent verification duty, confirmed vs disputed issue categories, synthesis result contract |\\n   366\\t| shared snippets | explicit placeholders, allowed runtime variables, and insertion order guarantees |\\n   367\\t\\n   368\\t### Prompt Assembly Pipeline\\n   369\\t\\n   370\\tPrompt assembly should be deterministic.\\n   371\\t\\n   372\\t1. Choose the role.\\n   373\\t2. Load that role’s base prompt.\\n   374\\t3. Append required reusable snippets for the operation.\\n   375\\t4. Append the relevant public insert file if present.\\n   376\\t5. Interpolate runtime values such as story path, tech-design shape, gate commands, and continuation handles.\\n   377\\t6. Emit one provider-ready prompt string and a compact assembly manifest for debugging.\\n   378\\t\\n   379\\t```typescript\\n   380\\texport interface PromptAssemblyInput {\\n   381\\t  role:\\n   382\\t    | \\\"story_implementor\\\"\\n   383\\t    | \\\"story_verifier\\\"\\n   384\\t    | \\\"quick_fixer\\\"\\n   385\\t    | \\\"epic_verifier\\\"\\n   386\\t    | \\\"epic_synthesizer\\\";\\n   387\\t  storyId?: string;\\n   388\\t  storyPath?: string;\\n   389\\t  epicPath: string;\\n   390\\t  techDesignPath: string;\\n   391\\t  techDesignCompanionPaths: string[];\\n   392\\t  testPlanPath: string;\\n   393\\t  implementationPromptInsertPath?: string;\\n   394\\t  verifierPromptInsertPath?: string;\\n   395\\t  gateCommands: {\\n   396\\t    story?: string;\\n   397\\t    epic?: string;\\n   398\\t  };\\n   399\\t  selfReviewPass?: number;\\n   400\\t  followupRequest?: string;\\n   401\\t}\\n   402\\t\\n   403\\texport interface PromptAssemblyResult {\\n   404\\t  prompt: string;\\n   405\\t  basePromptId: string;\\n   406\\t  snippetIds: string[];\\n   407\\t  publicInsertIds: string[];\\n   408\\t}\\n   409\\t```\\n   410\\t\\n   411\\tThe orchestrator does not need to see every runtime prompt asset, but the prompt-system reference should explain the mental model: base prompt plus snippets plus public insert plus runtime values.\\n   412\\t\\n   413\\tFor implementation, prompt assembly inputs should be modeled as discriminated unions by operation rather than as one giant loose object. The shared fields above describe the common shape; command-specific inputs should narrow them for:\\n   414\\t\\n   415\\t- story implement / continue\\n   416\\t- story verify\\n   417\\t- quick fix\\n   418\\t- epic cleanup\\n   419\\t- epic verify\\n   420\\t- epic synthesize\\n   421\\t\\n   422\\t## Flow 1: Inspect and Preflight\\n   423\\t\\n   424\\t**Covers:** AC-1.1, AC-1.2, AC-1.4, AC-1.5, AC-1.6, AC-2.1 to AC-2.4\\n   425\\t\\n   426\\tThis is the CLI’s setup boundary. `inspect` answers the structural question: is the spec pack arranged in a way the runtime understands? `preflight` answers the executable question: can this specific machine run the requested harness/model configuration with the required prompt assets and files in place?\\n   427\\t\\n   428\\tThis split matters. The orchestrator should be able to inspect a spec pack before it has chosen or authored a run config, but it should not launch any provider-backed work until preflight has passed.\\n   429\\t\\n   430\\t```mermaid\\n   431\\tsequenceDiagram\\n   432\\t    participant Orchestrator\\n   433\\t    participant CLI\\n   434\\t    participant SpecPack\\n   435\\t    participant Config as impl-run.config.json\\n   436\\t    participant Providers as Provider CLIs\\n   437\\t\\n   438\\t    Note over Orchestrator,Providers: AC-1.1 to AC-2.4\\n   439\\t    Orchestrator->>CLI: inspect --spec-pack-root <path>\\n   440\\t    CLI->>SpecPack: Resolve required files, story order, insert presence\\n   441\\t    CLI-->>Orchestrator: Spec-pack summary\\n   442\\t    Orchestrator->>CLI: preflight --spec-pack-root <path>\\n   443\\t    CLI->>Config: Parse and validate run config\\n   444\\t    CLI->>Providers: Validate requested harness availability\\n   445\\t    CLI-->>Orchestrator: Readiness report + validated provider matrix\\n   446\\t```\\n   447\\t\\n   448\\t### Inspect Result Contract\\n   449\\t\\n   450\\t```typescript\\n   451\\texport interface InspectResult {\\n   452\\t  status: \\\"ready\\\" | \\\"needs-user-decision\\\" | \\\"blocked\\\";\\n   453\\t  specPackRoot: string;\\n   454\\t  techDesignShape: \\\"two-file\\\" | \\\"four-file\\\";\\n   455\\t  artifacts: {\\n   456\\t    epicPath: string;\\n   457\\t    techDesignPath: string;\\n   458\\t    techDesignCompanionPaths: string[];\\n   459\\t    testPlanPath: string;\\n   460\\t    storiesDir: string;\\n   461\\t  };\\n   462\\t  stories: Array<{ id: string; title: string; path: string; order: number }>;\\n   463\\t  inserts: {\\n   464\\t    customStoryImplPromptInsert: \\\"present\\\" | \\\"absent\\\";\\n   465\\t    customStoryVerifierPromptInsert: \\\"present\\\" | \\\"absent\\\";\\n   466\\t  };\\n   467\\t  blockers: string[];\\n   468\\t  notes: string[];\\n   469\\t}\\n   470\\t```\\n   471\\t\\n   472\\t### Preflight Result Contract\\n   473\\t\\n   474\\t```typescript\\n   475\\texport interface PreflightResult {\\n   476\\t  status: \\\"ready\\\" | \\\"needs-user-decision\\\" | \\\"blocked\\\";\\n   477\\t  validatedConfig: ImplRunConfig;\\n   478\\t  providerMatrix: ProviderMatrix;\\n   479\\t  verificationGates?: {\\n   480\\t    storyGate: string;\\n   481\\t    epicGate: string;\\n   482\\t    source: string;\\n   483\\t  };\\n   484\\t  configValidationNotes: string[];\\n   485\\t  promptAssets: {\\n   486\\t    basePromptsReady: boolean;\\n   487\\t    snippetsReady: boolean;\\n   488\\t    notes: string[];\\n   489\\t  };\\n   490\\t  blockers: string[];\\n   491\\t  notes: string[];\\n   492\\t}\\n   493\\t```\\n   494\\t\\n   495\\t`preflight` validates the authored config and reports the consequences of that config. It does **not** generate or rewrite the default matrix in v1. Default selection is part of the skill/process contract and is owned by the orchestrator before preflight runs.\\n   496\\t\\n   497\\t### Story Ordering and Story ID Rules\\n   498\\t\\n   499\\tSpec-pack resolution must not invent story order heuristically.\\n   500\\t\\n   501\\t1. Read markdown files from `stories/`, excluding `coverage.md`.\\n   502\\t2. Derive `storyId` from the filename without extension.\\n   503\\t3. Order stories by the leading numeric prefix in the filename when present.\\n   504\\t4. If two files have the same numeric prefix, fall back to lexical filename order.\\n   505\\t5. If no numeric prefixes exist anywhere, use lexical filename order and emit a warning in `inspect`.\\n   506\\t6. If the naming pattern is mixed and produces ambiguous ordering, return `needs-user-decision`.\\n   507\\t\\n   508\\tThis keeps story order reproducible and makes the ordering rules explicit for both runtime code and tests.\\n   509\\t\\n   510\\tExamples:\\n   511\\t\\n   512\\t- `00-foundation.md`, `01-run-setup.md`, `02-prompts.md` -> valid, ordered numerically\\n   513\\t- `01-a.md`, `01-b.md` -> valid, ordered lexically within the same prefix\\n   514\\t- `01-a.md`, `b.md`, `02-c.md` -> ambiguous mixed pattern, return `needs-user-decision`\\n   515\\t\\n   516\\t### Tech Design Companion Discovery Rules\\n   517\\t\\n   518\\tThe four-file tech-design shape is valid only when:\\n   519\\t\\n   520\\t- `tech-design.md` exists\\n   521\\t- `test-plan.md` exists\\n   522\\t- exactly two additional files matching `tech-design-*.md` exist\\n   523\\t- `tech-design.md` itself is excluded from the companion count\\n   524\\t- companion paths are sorted lexically before being returned\\n   525\\t\\n   526\\tInvalid examples:\\n   527\\t\\n   528\\t- one companion file only\\n   529\\t- three or more companion files\\n   530\\t- unrelated files such as `tech-design-notes.md` or `tech-design-archive.md` inside the spec-pack root\\n   531\\t\\n   532\\tThese should return `blocked` with an explicit shape error rather than being ignored silently.\\n   533\\t\\n   534\\t### TC Mapping for This Flow\\n   535\\t\\n   536\\t| TC | Tests | Module | Setup | Assert |\\n   537\\t|---|---|---|---|---|\\n   538\\t| TC-1.1a | accepts two-file config | `inspect-command.test.ts` | fixture with `epic.md`, `tech-design.md`, `test-plan.md`, `stories/` | `inspect` returns `ready` and `two-file` |\\n   539\\t| TC-1.1b | accepts four-file config | `inspect-command.test.ts` | fixture with companions | `inspect` returns `ready` and `four-file` |\\n   540\\t| TC-1.1c | rejects `stories.md` layout | `inspect-command.test.ts` | invalid story layout fixture | `blocked` with explicit message |\\n   541\\t| TC-1.1d | rejects missing required artifact | `inspect-command.test.ts` | missing file fixture | `blocked` with named artifact |\\n   542\\t| TC-1.2a | records two-file config | `inspect-command.test.ts` | two-file fixture | tech-design shape recorded |\\n   543\\t| TC-1.2b | records four-file config | `inspect-command.test.ts` | four-file fixture | companions surfaced for later use |\\n   544\\t| TC-1.4a | detects both inserts | `inspect-command.test.ts` | fixture with both insert files | inserts marked `present` |\\n   545\\t| TC-1.4b | allows absent inserts | `inspect-command.test.ts` | fixture without insert files | inserts marked `absent`, no failure |\\n   546\\t| TC-1.5a | records full story order | `inspect-command.test.ts` | multi-story fixture | story inventory sorted and returned |\\n   547\\t| TC-1.6a | preflight carries discovered gates forward | `preflight-command.test.ts` | valid config + discovered gate inputs | readiness report includes gate-aware notes |\\n   548\\t| TC-1.6b | ambiguous gate policy returns `needs-user-decision` | `preflight-command.test.ts` | missing gate inputs | explicit pause state |\\n   549\\t| TC-2.1a | primary harness always available check runs | `preflight-command.test.ts` | primary harness fixture | provider matrix includes Claude Code |\\n   550\\t| TC-2.2a | Codex preferred when configured | `preflight-command.test.ts` | config using Codex | validated config preserves Codex role |\\n   551\\t| TC-2.2b | Copilot accepted when Codex absent and Copilot chosen | `preflight-command.test.ts` | config using Copilot | provider matrix validates Copilot path |\\n   552\\t| TC-2.2c | roles using `none` fall back to primary harness only | `preflight-command.test.ts` | config with `none` | no missing-secondary-harness error |\\n   553\\t| TC-2.3a-e | role model matrix validated | `config-schema.test.ts` | various valid configs | all role assignments parsed correctly |\\n   554\\t| TC-2.4a | validated run config returned for log capture | `preflight-command.test.ts` | valid fixture | structured `validatedConfig` returned |\\n   555\\t\\n   556\\t## Flow 2: Story Implementation and Continuation\\n   557\\t\\n   558\\t**Covers:** AC-3.1, AC-3.2, AC-3.4a, AC-4.1 to AC-4.5\\n   559\\t\\n   560\\t`story-implement` launches the implementor for one story using the assembled prompt and the configured role assignment. The CLI owns prompt assembly, provider invocation, self-review prompt evolution, and the returned structured result. It does not own story acceptance. `story-continue` exists because the story implementor may keep working in the same provider session across follow-up fixes or post-review continuation.\\n   561\\t\\n   562\\tThe most important boundary here is continuity. The CLI may continue a provider session, but it must do so only from explicit continuation/session handles emitted in the previous result and handed back by the orchestrator. The CLI should never try to recover the “latest” session implicitly.\\n   563\\t\\n   564\\t```mermaid\\n   565\\tsequenceDiagram\\n   566\\t    participant Orchestrator\\n   567\\t    participant CLI\\n   568\\t    participant Prompt as Prompt Assembly\\n   569\\t    participant Adapter as Provider Adapter\\n   570\\t    participant Harness as Secondary or Primary Harness\\n   571\\t\\n   572\\t    Note over Orchestrator,Harness: AC-3.1 to AC-4.5\\n   573\\t    Orchestrator->>CLI: story-implement --story-id <id>\\n   574\\t    CLI->>Prompt: Build implementor prompt\\n   575\\t    Prompt-->>CLI: assembled prompt + manifest\\n   576\\t    CLI->>Adapter: run implementor role\\n   577\\t    Adapter->>Harness: execute prompt\\n   578\\t    Harness-->>Adapter: result + session handle\\n   579\\t    CLI->>Prompt: run self-review pass 1..N\\n   580\\t    CLI-->>Orchestrator: structured implementor result + continuation handle\\n   581\\t```\\n   582\\t\\n   583\\t### Continuation Handle Contract\\n   584\\t\\n   585\\t```typescript\\n   586\\texport interface ContinuationHandle {\\n   587\\t  provider: \\\"claude-code\\\" | \\\"codex\\\" | \\\"copilot\\\";\\n   588\\t  sessionId: string;\\n   589\\t  storyId: string;\\n   590\\t}\\n   591\\t```\\n   592\\t\\n   593\\t### Implementor Result Contract\\n   594\\t\\n   595\\t```typescript\\n   596\\texport interface ImplementorResult {\\n   597\\t  resultId: string;\\n   598\\t  provider: \\\"claude-code\\\" | \\\"codex\\\" | \\\"copilot\\\";\\n   599\\t  model: string;\\n   600\\t  role: \\\"story_implementor\\\";\\n   601\\t  sessionId?: string;\\n   602\\t  outcome:\\n   603\\t    | \\\"ready-for-verification\\\"\\n   604\\t    | \\\"needs-followup-fix\\\"\\n   605\\t    | \\\"needs-human-ruling\\\"\\n   606\\t    | \\\"blocked\\\";\\n   607\\t  continuation?: ContinuationHandle;\\n   608\\t  story: {\\n   609\\t    id: string;\\n   610\\t    title: string;\\n   611\\t  };\\n   612\\t  planSummary: string;\\n   613\\t  changedFiles: Array<{ path: string; reason: string }>;\\n   614\\t  tests: {\\n   615\\t    added: string[];\\n   616\\t    modified: string[];\\n   617\\t    removed: string[];\\n   618\\t    totalAfterStory?: number;\\n   619\\t    deltaFromPriorBaseline?: number;\\n   620\\t  };\\n   621\\t  gatesRun: Array<{ command: string; result: \\\"pass\\\" | \\\"fail\\\" | \\\"not-run\\\" }>;\\n   622\\t  selfReview: {\\n   623\\t    passesRun: number;\\n   624\\t    findingsFixed: string[];\\n   625\\t    findingsSurfaced: string[];\\n   626\\t  };\\n   627\\t  openQuestions: string[];\\n   628\\t  specDeviations: string[];\\n   629\\t  recommendedNextStep: string;\\n   630\\t}\\n   631\\t```\\n   632\\t\\n   633\\t### Self-Review Rules\\n   634\\t\\n   635\\tThe CLI should own the mechanical shape of self-review:\\n   636\\t\\n   637\\t- Pass 1: broad correctness and obvious omissions\\n   638\\t- Pass 2: test/coverage and requirement alignment\\n   639\\t- Pass 3: residual risk and boundary audit\\n   640\\t\\n   641\\tStop rules:\\n   642\\t\\n   643\\t- stop early if the provider reports a blocking condition\\n   644\\t- stop early if a pass surfaces only human-ruling issues\\n   645\\t- otherwise run the configured number of passes\\n   646\\t\\n   647\\t### TC Mapping for This Flow\\n   648\\t\\n   649\\t| TC | Tests | Module | Setup | Assert |\\n   650\\t|---|---|---|---|---|\\n   651\\t| TC-3.1a | implementor prompt assembly uses base + snippets | `prompt-assembly.test.ts` | implementor input fixture | assembly includes correct base prompt and snippets |\\n   652\\t| TC-3.1b | verifier prompt assembly uses base + snippets | `prompt-assembly.test.ts` | verifier input fixture | verifier assets selected correctly |\\n   653\\t| TC-3.2a | implementor insert applied when present | `prompt-assembly.test.ts` | insert file fixture | insert content included every run |\\n   654\\t| TC-3.2b | implementor insert omitted cleanly when absent | `prompt-assembly.test.ts` | no insert file | no failure path and no insert ID |\\n   655\\t| TC-3.4a | implementor reading journey included | `prompt-assembly.test.ts` | implementor input | reading-journey snippet and paths included |\\n   656\\t| TC-4.1a | story implement uses documented contract | `story-implement-command.test.ts` | valid config + story fixture | command returns structured implementor result |\\n   657\\t| TC-4.2a | self-review remains in same session | `provider-adapter.test.ts` | fake provider session | self-review They should not reuse prior verifier sessions. `quick-fix` is intentionally lighter than a full implementor pass. It exists for small, bounded corrections that do not justify re-running the full story implementation workflow.\\n   670\\t\\n   671\\tThe orchestration rule here is simple. The CLI can execute verification or a quick fix, but it cannot decide that a story is accepted. It only returns disciplined findings and outcomes. guide fixture | public docs state gate is orchestrator-owned |\\n   783\\t\\n   784\\t## Flow 4: Epic Cleanup, Verification, and Synthesis\\n   785\\t\\n   786\\t**Covers:** AC-7.1 to AC-7.3, AC-8.1 to AC-8.4\\n   787\\t\\n   788\\tAt epic closeout the CLI executes three different kinds of work: cleanup-only correction, epic-level verifier batches, and synthesis. The orchestrator still determines whether the run can close. The CLI should never blur these responsibilities into one vague “finish the epic” command.\\n   789\\t\\n   790\\tThe cleanup operation is distinct because it acts on an orchestrator-curated cleanup batch. It is not a general-purpose retry. It is a bounded correction-only workflow designed to start epic verification from the cleaned state rather than from an accumulation of known-but-deferred issues.\\n   791\\t\\n   792\\t```mermaid\\n   793\\tsequenceDiagram\\n   794\\t    participant Orchestrator\\n   795\\t    participant CLI\\n   796\\t    participant Cleanup as Cleanup Runner\\n   797\\t    participant EV as Epic Verifier Batch\\n   798\\t    participant SYN as Epic Synthesizer\\n   799\\t\\n   800\\t    Note over Orchestrator,SYN: AC-7.1 to AC-8.4\\n   801\\t    Orchestrator->>CLI: epic-cleanup --batch <cleanup.md>\\n   802\\t    CLI->>Cleanup: apply approved cleanup items\\n   803\\t    Cleanup-->>CLI: cleanup result\\n   804\\t    CLI-->>Orchestrator: cleanup outcome\\n   805\\t    Orchestrator->>CLI: epic-verify\\n   806\\t    CLI->>EV: launch epic verifiers\\n   807\\t    EV-->>CLI: epic verifier results\\n   808\\t    CLI-->>Orchestrator: verifier batch\\n   809\\t    Orchestrator->>CLI: epic-synthesize\\n   810\\t    CLI->>SYN: verify and consolidate findings\\n   811\\t    SYN-->>CLI: synthesis result\\n   812\\t    CLI-->>Orchestrator: synthesis outcome\\n   813\\t```\\n   814\\t\\n   815\\t### Epic Verifier Result\\n   816\\t\\n   817\\t```typescript\\n   818\\texport interface EpicVerifierResult {\\n   819\\t  resultId: string;\\n   820\\t  outcome: \\\"pass\\\" | \\\"revise\\\" | \\\"block\\\";\\n   821\\t  provider: \\\"claude-code\\\" | \\\"codex\\\" | \\\"copilot\\\";\\n   822\\t  model: string;\\n   823\\t  reviewerLabel: string;\\n   824\\t  crossStoryFindings: string[];\\n   825\\t  architectureFindings: string[];\\n   826\\t  epicCoverageAssessment: string[];\\n   827\\t  mockOrShimAuditFindings: string[];\\n   828\\t  blockingFindings:  833\\t```\\n   834\\t\\n   835\\t### Epic Cleanup Result\\n   836\\t\\n   837\\t```typescript\\n   838\\texport interface EpicCleanupResult {\\n   839\\t  resultId: string;\\n   840\\t  outcome: 849\\t\\n   850\\t### Epic Synthesis Result\\n   851\\t\\n   852\\t```typescript\\n   853\\texport interface EpicSynthesisResult {\\n   854\\t  resultId: string;\\n   855\\t  outcome:\\n   856\\t    | \\\"ready-for-closeout\\\"\\n   857\\t    | \\\"needs-fixes\\\"\\n   858\\t    | \\\"needs-more-verification\\\"\\n   859\\t    | \\\"blocked\\\";\\n   860\\t  confirmedIssues: string[];\\n   861\\t guide fixture | docs state final gate is outside CLI |\\n   880\\t\\n   881\\t## Provider Adapters\\n   882\\t\\n   883\\tThe provider adapters let the CLI execute the same operation contract against different harnesses without changing the orchestrator-facing command surface.\\n   884\\t\\n   885\\t```typescript\\n   886\\texport interface ProviderExecutionRequest {\\n   887\\t  prompt: string;\\n   888\\t  cwd: string;\\n   889\\t  model: string;\\n   890\\t  reasoningEffort: string;\\n   891\\t  resumeSessionId?: string;\\n   892\\t  timeoutMs: number;\\n   893\\t}\\n   894\\t\\n   895\\texport interface ProviderExecutionResult {\\n   896\\t  provider: \\\"claude-code\\\" | \\\"codex\\\" | \\\"copilot\\\";\\n   897\\t  sessionId?: string;\\n   898\\t  stdout: string;\\n   899\\t  stderr: string;\\n   900\\t  exitCode: number;\\n   901\\t  parsedResult?: unknown;\\n   902\\t}\\n   903\\t\\n   904\\texport interface ProviderAdapter {\\n   905\\t  checkAvailability(): Promise<HarnessAvailability>;\\n   906\\t  execute(request: ProviderExecutionRequest): Promise<ProviderExecutionResult>;\\n   907\\t}\\n   908\\t```\\n   909\\t\\n   910\\tAdapter rules:\\n   911\\t\\n   912\\t- `claude-code` adapter uses the primary harness path and supports “no secondary harness” roles.\\n   913\\t- `codex` adapter supports runtime model and reasoning-effort overrides.\\n   914\\t- `copilot` adapter supports runtime model override, but reasoning effort is effectively config-level and should be treated as advisory in validation notes.\\n   915\\t- Adapters must return explicit session IDs when the provider exposes them. The CLI should never rely on “latest session for cwd” behavior.\\n   916\\t\\n   917\\t### Provider Invocation Contracts\\n   918\\t\\n   919\\tThe adapter behavior must be explicit per harness.\\n   920\\t\\n   921\\t| Harness | Invocation Shape | Resume Shape | Notes |\\n   922\\t|---|---|---|---|\\n   923\\t| `codex` | `codex exec --json -m <model> -c model_reasoning_effort=<effort> \\\"<prompt>\\\"` | `codex exec resume --json <session-id> \\\"<follow-up prompt>\\\"` | use explicit session IDs only; capture stdout to artifact before parsing |\\n   924\\t| `copilot` | `copilot -p \\\"<prompt>\\\" -s --model <model> ...` with adapter-owned result capture and permission flags | no session resume contract for retained implementor in v1; use fresh programmatic runs only | Copilot is valid for fresh-session roles only in v1; reasoning effort treated as advisory because it is config-level |\\n   925\\t| `claude-code` | `claude -p \\\"<prompt>\\\" --output-format json --model <model> --permission-mode <mode>` | `claude -p \\\"<follow-up prompt>\\\" --resume <session-id> --output-format json --model <model>` | do not reuse the live interactive orchestrator session; do not rely on `--continue` or latest-session-by-cwd behavior |\\n   926\\t\\n   927\\tThe v1 design assumes the primary harness is still invokable as a child process. If that proves false in implementation, the design must be updated explicitly rather than allowing the runtime model to drift into “emit a prompt and ask the orchestrator to run it manually.”\\n   928\\t\\n   929\\t### `story-continue` Resolution Rules\\n   930\\t\\n   931\\t`story-continue` resolves `model` and `reasoningEffort` from the active `story_implementor` entry in the selected config file unless a future continuation-handle schema explicitly carries immutable model metadata. The continuation handle is intentionally light in v1 and should not become a second config surface.\\n   932\\t\\n   933\\t### Availability and Auth Checks\\n   934\\t\\n   935\\t| Harness | Version Check | Auth Check | Availability Tier Notes |\\n   936\\t|---|---|---|---|\\n   937\\t| `claude-code` | `claude --version` | `claude auth status` | supports `binary-present` and `authenticated-known` tiers |\\n   938\\t| `codex` | `codex --version` | explicit auth-state command if available, otherwise mark auth as `unknown` without paid smoke by default | supports `binary-present`; `authenticated-known` only if a non-mutating auth check exists |\\n   939\\t| `copilot` | `copilot --version` | explicit auth-state command if available, otherwise mark auth as `unknown` without paid smoke by default | `--npx` fallback deferred in v1 |\\n   940\\t\\n   941\\tAvailability tiers:\\n   942\\t\\n   943\\t- `binary-present`\\n   944\\t- `authenticated-known`\\n   945\\t- `auth-unknown`\\n   946\\t- `unavailable`\\n   947\\t\\n   948\\t### Provider Output Parsing Rules\\n   949\\t\\n   950\\t- Provider stdout is captured in full to a temp/result artifact before parsing.\\n   951\\t- The adapter validates the parsed provider result before converting it into the command result envelope.\\n   952\\t- Invalid structured output becomes a machine-readable CLI error with a stable error code.\\n   953\\t- Missing session IDs are treated as blocking for commands that require continuation support.\\n   954\\t- Provider stderr may be persisted for debugging, but it is not the orchestration source of truth.\\n   955\\t\\n   956\\tFor v1, provider prompts require the model to return exactly one JSON object matching the operation payload schema. The adapter accepts only:\\n   957\\t\\n   958\\t1. provider-native JSON whose final text/result field is exactly that object, or\\n   959\\t2. raw stdout that is exactly that object.\\n   960\\t\\n   961\\tThe adapter does not parse arbitrary prose, markdown summaries, or fenced JSON blocks in v1. Invalid output returns `PROVIDER_OUTPUT_INVALID`.\\n   962\\t\\n   963\\t## Verification Gate Resolution\\n   964\\t\\n   965\\tThe runtime must not guess gate commands ad hoc.\\n   966\\t\\n   967\\t1. explicit command flags\\n   968\\t2. explicit run-config values, if later added\\n   969\\t3. discovered package scripts\\n   970\\t4. supporting policy docs or CI config\\n   971\\t5. otherwise `needs-user-decision`\\n   972\\t\\n   973\\t```typescript\\n   974\\texport interface VerificationGateConfig {\\n   975\\t  storyGate: string;\\n   976\\t  epicGate: string;\\n   977\\t  source: \\\"cli-flag\\\" | \\\"run-config\\\" | \\\"package-scripts\\\" | \\\"policy-doc\\\" | \\\"ci-config\\\";\\n   978\\t}\\n   979\\t```\\n   980\\t\\n   981\\tThe preflight result should surface this config so the orchestrator can persist it into `team-impl-log.md`.\\n   982\\t\\n   983\\t### Canonical Gate Mapping\\n   984\\t\\n   985\\tUntil a project overrides the defaults, the expected mapping for this repo is:\\n   986\\t\\n   987\\t| Gate | Command |\\n   988\\t|---|---|\\n   989\\t| Story gate | `bun run green-verify` |\\n   990\\t| Epic gate | `bun run verify-all` |\\n   991\\t\\n   992\\tThis mapping should appear consistently in:\\n   993\\t\\n   994\\t- `preflight` output\\n   995\\t- `team-impl-log.md`\\n   996\\t- prompt assembly inputs\\n   997\\t- process-playbook examples\\n   998\\t\\n   999\\t## Packaging and Build Integration\\n  1000\\t\\n  1001\\tThe source runtime stays modular. The packaged skill should contain one bundled Node-compatible JS file.\\n  1002\\t\\n  1003\\t### Build Steps\\n  1004\\t\\n  1005\\t1. Author prompt assets under `processes/impl-cli/prompts/`.\\n  1006\\t2. Run `scripts/sync-impl-cli-assets.ts` to generate `embedded-assets.generated.ts`.\\n  1007\\t3. Bundle `processes/impl-cli/cli.ts` with Bun targeting Node-compatible output.\\n  1008\\t4. Copy the bundled artifact into `dist/skills/ls-claude-impl/bin/`.\\n  1009\\t5. Validate that the packaged skill includes `SKILL.md`, all reference files, and the runtime artifact.\\n  1010\\t\\n  1011\\t### Bundling Notes\\n  1012\\t\\n  1013\\t- Keep the build target Node-compatible.\\n  1014\\t- Prefer a single JS runtime artifact in the packaged skill.\\n  1015\\t- Embed prompt assets into the bundle to avoid distributing a second runtime prompt tree inside the skill.\\n  1016\\t- Leave provider CLIs external. They are environment prerequisites, not bundled dependencies.\\n  1017\\t\\n  1018\\t### Build Commands\\n  1019\\t\\n  1020\\t```bash\\n  1021\\t# 1. regenerate embedded prompt assets\\n  1022\\tbun run scripts/sync-impl-cli-assets.ts\\n  1023\\t\\n  1024\\t# 2. bundle node-compatible runtime artifact\\n  1025\\tbun build processes/impl-cli/cli.ts \\\\\\n  1026\\t  --target=node \\\\\\n  1027\\t  --format=cjs \\\\\\n  1028\\t  --outfile dist/generated/ls-impl-cli.cjs\\n  1029\\t```\\n  1030\\t\\n  1031\\tThe build pipeline should then copy `dist/generated/ls-impl-cli.cjs` into `dist/skills/ls-claude-impl/bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs` and fail validation if that file is missing.\\n  1032\\t\\n  1033\\t### Artifact Persistence Layout\\n  1034\\t\\n  1035\\tThe CLI should write result artifacts automatically under the spec-pack root so recovery does not depend on stdout capture alone.\\n  1036\\t\\n  1037\\t```text\\n  1038\\t<spec-pack-root>/artifacts/\\n  1039\\t├── story-03/\\n  1040\\t│   ├── 001-implementor.json\\n  1041\\t│   ├── 002-verifier-batch.json\\n  1042\\t│   ├── 003-quick-fix.json\\n  1043\\t│   └── 004-reverify-batch.json\\n  1044\\t├── cleanup/\\n  1045\\t│   └── 001-cleanup-result.json\\n  1046\\t└── epic/\\n  1047\\t    ├── 001-epic-verifier-batch.json\\n  1048\\t    └── 002-epic-synthesis.json\\n  1049\\t```\\n  1050\\t\\n  1051\\tCommand stdout should still emit the JSON result envelope, but the CLI should also persist the same envelope to the artifact path it returns. That keeps orchestration and recovery deterministic.\\n  1052\\t\\n  1053\\t### Packaging Decisions\\n  1054\\t\\n  1055\\t- Ship CommonJS at `bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs` in v1.\\n  1056\\t- Defer Copilot `--npx` fallback from v1. Preflight should validate directly available Copilot CLI support only for explicitly Copilot-backed roles.\\n  1057\\t\\n  1058\\t## Security and Operational Guardrails\\n  1059\\t\\n  1060\\tThis runtime reads project files and launches external CLIs. GPT model lanes depend on whether Codex CLI or Copilot CLI is available in the local environment.\\n\\n**Objective**\\n\\nExecute one story implementation call through the CLI, keep the implementor in a retained session for that story, run evolving self-review passes inside the same session, apply only non-controversial fixes automatically, and return a structured result that the orchestrator can route and persist.\\n\\n**In Scope**\\n\\n- Story implementor launch through the CLI\\n- Retained-session continuity for implementor + self-review\\n- Three-pass self-review with evolving prompts\\n- Automatic application of non-controversial fixes only\\n- Structured implementor result with continuation/session fields\\n\\n**Out of Scope**\\n\\n- Fresh verifier batch execution\\n- Quick-fix workflow\\n- Story acceptance and progression\\n- Resume from interruption after the orchestrator session is lost\\n\\n**Dependencies**\\n\\n- Story 2 complete\\n- Prompt assembly and reading-journey contracts available\\n- Run config resolved and provider availability confirmed\\n\\n### Acceptance Criteria\\n<!-- Jira: Acceptance Criteria field -->\\n\\n**AC-4.1:** Story implementation is launched through a documented CLI contract\\n\\n- **TC-4.1a: Implementor launch**\\n  - Given: The orchestrator is ready to start a story\\n  - When: It calls the story implementor CLI operation\\n  - Then: The CLI accepts the documented inputs, reads the specified spec-pack artifacts, and returns a structured implementation result\\n\\n**AC-4.2:** The story implementor remains a retained session for the duration of the story workflow unless replaced\\n\\n- **TC-4.2a: Same-session continuity**\\n  - Given: Story implementation completes successfully\\n  - When: Self-review begins\\n  - Then: Self-review runs in the same implementor session rather than a fresh session\\n- **TC-4.2b: Session continuation fields returned**\\n  - Given: The CLI finishes a story implementation call\\n  - When: It returns the structured result\\n  - Then: The result includes the provider identifier and session identifier required to continue that implementor session later\\n\\n**AC-4.3:** Default self-review uses three passes with evolving prompts\\n\\n- **TC-4.3a: Three self-review passes**\\n  - Given: A story implementation call reaches self-review\\n  - When: The CLI runs the default self-review cycle\\n  - Then: It runs three self-review passes unless the workflow stops early because of a blocking condition\\n- **TC-4.3b: Evolving self-review prompt**\\n  - Given: The CLI is running multiple self-review passes\\n  - When: It advances from one pass to the next\\n  - Then: Each pass adjusts the self-review prompt so the implementor does not repeat the same review request unchanged\\n\\n**AC-4.4:** Self-review applies only non-controversial fixes automatically\\n\\n- **TC-4.4a: Non-controversial fix**\\n  - Given: A self-review pass finds a clear and local correction with no meaningful design ambiguity\\n  - When: The implementor can fix it confidently\\n  - Then: The CLI allows that fix to be applied during the same story implementation call\\n- **TC-4.4b: Uncertain fix**\\n  - Given: A self-review pass finds a potential issue but the implementor is not confident the change is the right fix\\n  - When: The self-review cycle completes\\n  - Then: The issue is surfaced to the orchestrator for routing instead of being fixed automatically\\n\\n**AC-4.5:** The implementor returns a structured report suitable for orchestration and later verification\\n\\n- **TC-4.5a: Structured implementation result**\\n  - Given: Story implementation and self-review complete\\n  - When: The CLI returns the implementor result\\n  - Then: The result includes identity fields, planning summary, changed files, tests added or changed, gates run, self-review findings, open questions, any spec deviations, and a recommended next step\\n\\n### Technical Design\\n<!-- Jira: Technical Notes or sub-section of Description -->\\n\\n**Relevant Data Contracts**\\n\\n#### CLI Operations\\n\\n| Operation | Purpose | Required Inputs | Expected Outputs | Outcome States |\\n|-----------|---------|-----------------|------------------|----------------|\\n| Launch story implementor | Start story implementation | story id, role config, prompt assembly inputs, working directory | structured implementor result, provider id, session id | ready-for-verification, needs-followup-fix, needs-human-ruling, blocked |\\n| Continue story implementor | Continue the retained implementor session | provider id, session id, follow-up prompt contract | continued implementor result | ready-for-verification, needs-followup-fix, needs-human-ruling, blocked |\\n\\n#### Implementor Result\\n\\n| Field | Description |\\n|-------|-------------|\\n| provider id | Provider/runtime used for the implementor |\\n| session id | Identifier required to continue the summary | Tests added, modified, removed, and total count after story |\\n| test-count delta | Change in test total from the prior accepted story baseline |\\n| gates run and gate results | What was executed and what passed or failed |\\n| self-review findings fixed | Issues found and fixed during the self-review loop |\\n| self-review findings surfaced | Uncertain or unresolved issues sent back The JSON payload remains the source of truth.\\n\\n| Exit Code | Meaning |\\n|---|---|\\n| `0` | Command completed and produced a non-blocking outcome (`ready`, `ready-for-verification`, `pass`, `cleaned`, `ready-for-closeout`) |\\n| `2` | Command completed but requires follow-up (`needs-user-decision`, `needs-followup-fix`, `needs-human-ruling`, `revise`, `needs-more-routing`, `needs-more-cleanup`, `needs-fixes`, `needs-more-verification`) |\\n| `3` | Command is blocked by environment, spec-pack, config, or provider readiness issue |\\n| `1` | Invalid invocation, schema parse failure, or uncaught runtime error |\\n\\n### Result Routing Matrix\\n\\nThis matrix is authoritative for handler implementation and tests.\\n\\n| Command Outcome | Envelope Status | Exit Code | Orchestrator Action |\\n|---|---|---:|---|\\n| `ready` | `ok` | 0 | proceed to next setup step |\\n| `ready-for-verification` | `ok` | 0 | run verification or next bounded step |\\n| `pass` | `ok` | 0 | evaluate for acceptance or next stage |\\n| `cleaned` | `ok` | 0 | move into epic verification |\\n| `ready-for-closeout` | `ok` | 0 | run final orchestrator-owned gate |\\n| `needs-user-decision` | `needs-user-decision` | 2 | pause and request human clarification |\\n| `needs-followup-fix` | `ok` | 2 | route same-session follow-up or fix path |\\n| `needs-human-ruling` | `needs-user-decision` | 2 | pause for orchestrator/human ruling |\\n| `revise` | `ok` | 2 | route fix and rerun verification |\\n| `needs-more-routing` | `ok` | 2 | select another bounded correction path |\\n| `needs-more-cleanup` | `ok` | 2 | continue cleanup cycle |\\n| `needs-fixes` | `ok` | 2 | route fixes before closeout |\\n| `needs-more-verification` | `ok` | 2 | launch additional verification/synthesis |\\n| `blocked` | `blocked` | 3 | stop and inspect blocking error details |\\n| parse/schema/runtime failure | `error` | 1 | treat as CLI failure rather than feature-state
